text:
# Keep competitors at bay with patents

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Industry](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/industrie/index.html)

04/11/2025 • 3 minutes reading

[David Kemps](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/david-kemps/index.html)

How innovative are the companies in The Makers Top 100 based on their patents? And why is applying for patents important for manufacturing companies?

“Trade secrets are only valuable if you really protect them,” says Marco Coolen, European patent attorney and partner at AOMB. 'A patent is often a silent force. It's frightening. But ultimately the judge determines how strong your patent really is.'

[Read also: Top 100 makers account for 102 billion turnover and 300,000 jobs](/nl/business/insights/sectors-and-trends/industry/makers-top-100-account-for-102-billion-turnover-and-300,000-jobs.html)

## Manufacturing companies should check whether their IP is properly protected

Especially for Link Magazine, Marco Coolen and David Kemps from ABN AMRO analyzed the patent applications of companies from the first Makers Top 100. "The positive news: 63 companies from the list have done something with patents in recent years," says Coolen. 'About fifteen companies submit applications on a regular basis, a group among them does so occasionally, and some have only one or a few applications to their name.'

Companies that apply for patents often have their own products. The larger companies employ more R&D people, which result in more patents. On average, in the Netherlands there are seven patent applications per hundred R&D people. Not surprisingly, companies such as Philips, NXP, Signify and ASML top the list. Names such as VDL and Lely also stand out. “These companies often employ their own patent attorneys because of the number of patent applications,” Coolen says. 'Patent attorneys make it easier to submit applications and use patents strategically. This in turn has a strengthening effect on the number of patent applications, causing the distance to the rest of the top 25 to grow rapidly.'

## What does a patent attorney do and how long is a patent valid?

A patent attorney is legally authorized to apply for, file and process patents on behalf of companies or their clients – nationally and internationally. He or she advises on how best to protect an invention, thinks strategically about the formulation of claims and supervises the entire application process.

A standard patent is valid for twenty years from the date of filing. The condition is that maintenance costs are paid annually.

## Keep competition at bay

Why are patents important for manufacturing companies? “Patents can protect an innovation for twenty years,” Coolen explains. 'This allows you to keep competitors at bay or generate income by issuing licenses. Companies such as Philips and Signify have a lot of experience with licenses.' Coolen therefore advocates that manufacturing companies check for themselves whether their IP is well protected. According to Kemps, patents are also valuable for investors. 'They provide legal certainty about the uniqueness of products or techniques. That makes it more attractive to finance.'

[Read also: Family businesses and private equity put their own stamp on industry](/nl/business/insights/sectors-and-trends/industry/family-businesses-and-private-equity-put-own-stamp-on-industrie.html)

## How do you write a strong patent application?

- Provide a truly new and innovative invention
- Have the application drawn up and checked by a patent attorney.
- Pay close attention to the conclusions (claims) – they determine the scope of your protection.
- Think ahead: how would a competitor try to work around it?
- Think strategically: do you only want protection in the Netherlands or also in the EU or beyond?
- Only make your invention/innovation public after submission

## Patents not always attractive

Kemps does make an important comment. 'It is not the case that patents are attractive for all innovations. Manufacturing companies without their own products also innovate, for example in internal processes. Keeping these innovations secret is often more attractive. With a patent you publish your innovation. Your competitor can then build on this.' Still, the research into the Makers Top 100 offers interesting insights, according to Kemps. 'In addition to the usual suspects such as ASML and Philips, there are also less obvious names. I am curious to see how these companies will develop further.'

The industry is a heavyweight in the Dutch economy. Export is an important driver for this, but at the same time creates uncertainty. There are also themes that have a positive influence on growth, such as cooperation in the chain, innovation and sustainability.
